scenario: A claims-processing BPMN is owned by an insurance company. The diagram should clearly show that the company interacts with a customer Pool (sending requests, receiving documents) but the team doesn't model the customer's internal process at all -- the customer is an external participant whose internal steps are out of scope. The team wonders how to represent the customer Pool without modelling its insides.
question: Which BPMN pattern fits "external participant Pool, internals not modelled"?

options.text: Use a regular Pool with inner placeholder Service Tasks to suggest the customer's actions. This approach is problematic because modelling assumed steps for an external party is speculative and misrepresents the model. It may mislead readers into thinking the team knows the customer's internal process when they do not. BPMN provides a better mechanism -- the black-box Pool -- for expressing exactly this "external participant, internals unknown" scenario clearly and accurately.

Use a "black-box" Pool (also called a "collapsed Pool") for the customer -- a Pool drawn as an empty rectangle without internal flow content. Only the Message Flows crossing into/out of it are visible. " Standard BPMN convention for external participants whose internals are out of scope. In Zeebe-deployed collaboration diagrams, the black-box Pool is not executable.

Use a Lane within the company's Pool labelled "Customer" to represent the external party. This approach has the wrong scope because Lanes are internal organisational units used to subdivide a single participant's Pool, such as departments or roles within one company. Lanes do not represent separate external participants. Using a Lane for an external party incorrectly places the customer inside the company's process boundary and obscures the inter-participant interaction model.

Use a text annotation labelled "Customer" to indicate the external participant in the diagram. They carry no formal interaction semantics and do not support Message Flow connections that express inter-participant communication. Readers would see a comment, not a formal collaboration participant, and the model would lack the structural information about who sends and receives messages.

a.text: Incorrect. The black-box Pool (collapsed Pool) is BPMN's standard convention for representing external participants whose internal process is out of scope. The Pool is drawn as an empty rectangle with just a name (e.g., "Customer"). Message Flows (dashed lines with envelope marker) cross into / out of it, formally declaring the inter-participant interactions. Readers immediately understand: "we know this participant exists, we model the messages exchanged, but we don't claim to know their internal process." In Zeebe-deployed BPMN, the black-box Pool isn't an executable process (you only set isExecutable=true on the company's Pool). The customer Pool is purely descriptive -- gives shape to the inter-participant story.

b.text: Correct. Inventing inner steps for an external participant is speculative and may mislead. The black-box convention is precisely for "we don't know / don't model."

c.text: Incorrect. Lanes are intra-Pool sub-divisions for organisational units within one company. External participants belong in their own Pool.

d.text: Incorrect. Annotations are documentation comments; Message Flows carry the formal interaction semantics.

explanation: Inventing inner steps for an external participant is speculative and may mislead. The black-box convention is precisely for "we don't know / don't model."
